sentences.text: Well, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had very two Jewish good friends from high school, with whom I was not friendly in high school because they were Jewish.
But I became very friendly with one of them especially.
And she was really the one-- she and her husband now-- brought me in into the Jewish circle of academia in-- in Breslau.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I-- as far as the university itself is concerned, there were a few Jews there.
And I had a few-- I had some Polish friends.
And at that time, I was a Jew.

I was not hiding my identity, and I was-- I felt much better about it, because I was not afraid that somebody, you know, behind my back was saying, oh, look at this-- look at this Jewish girl that goes to church, or whatever.
I don't remember any incidents.
I think some of them were really nice.
Some of the Polish friends were very nice.
I remember one time that I lost my roommate and I had no place to live, and one of the Polish women took me into her [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] room in-- what do you call it?
The-- into the housing-- A dormitory?
Into dormitory, and shared her bed with me or whatever with me also for a couple of weeks.
So they were nice.
I don't remember any-- any problems, anything.

The problems that were were the political problems really more so, because you were really scared and afraid that somebody will say, well, you are anti-Communist and you are this and you are that because, you know, at that time, I mean, Stalin's grip of Poland was really terrible and you were afraid of your own shadow.
And, you know, you never knew whether they were going to throw you out or put you to jail or-- or whatever.
But they were quite happy days of my life, you know?
I was-- All of a sudden, you were going to dances and you were trying to find, you know, some romance, some mate.
So you were dating.
I was dating.
I had a, you know, circle of friends.
Where did you meet your husband?
Oh, where.
I met him in Breslau.
He.
He.
Yes.
To Israel.
Yes, well, that was-- by then it was 1956, and that was after the Hungarian revolution, and after-- and that was during the time Gomulka came to power in Poland-- became the Prime Minister.
And he opened the borders and told the Jews, if you want to leave, you can leave.
So-- so we left.
So he made me leave, really, because I really didn't want-- I didn't want to leave.
I didn't want to leave my parents behind, and my sister behind.
Yes, I did.
Yes.
What had happened was my sister was in medical school at that time and she had-- well, she had just started really.
And my-- my mother, after I-- after we left-- when she saw that there was no way that I was going to stay, because she was very much against my leaving-- they applied for a visa, too.
And they were ready to go.
They got the visa.
They got passport.
They were everything.
We came to Israel at that time.
And this was during the Sinai War, and it was very tough in Israel.
The economic situation was really hard, tough, et cetera.
And my stepfather really didn't want to leave because, as I said, he was very assimilated.
That was very far to him, and very strange and alien.
So my mother was really take-- undertaking the responsibility of them leaving.
And we were writing-- and I can blame myself for that-- that I was writing very bad letters, that it's very hard and the situation is tough.
And-- and I think, because of that, they remained.
They turned back in the papers and whatever, and they remained.
Two years.
Well, I went to Ulpan and-- for half a year and I learned Hebrew.
And after that, I got a job as an economist in an Israeli firm, and I was working.
My husband.
I didn't want to leave Israel.
I was very happy there.
I mean, in the beginning, I was miserable.
I was, you know, missing my-- I was very close with my mother, very close.
And I still remember the pain I had when I was saying goodbye to her.
It was like a physical, physical pain, like somebody took a knife and cut my heart.
That's how painful that was.
So I was really missing her and I was missing my sister, my stepfather.
And, I guess, the whole culture.
It was very strange.
But after half a year or a year, I really got acclimated there.
And I had a job and we had a-- we got a very nice apartment.
I had a lot of family in Israel from my father's side, and all our friends came, little by little, to Israel.
So we really had a nice-- very nice social life, everything.
But my husband's family was here in America-- his father and his sister and his brother-in-law-- and he decided that there was not enough room for him to grow in Israel, and he begged me to go.
And I really didn't want to, but he put enough pressure that I finally agreed.
His family.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for-- at the beginning, I went and I met-- I mean, somebody suggested I should go to Burrough's school.
Burroughs Corporation was training people, giving courses for machine operators.
They were bookkeeping machine operators.
Being that my English was practically nil, I went, I took the course and I finished, you know, before everybody else.
And I got a very good job on Wall Street with some lawyer who was having a [INAUDIBLE] et cetera.
And I was working for him for about a year, a year and a half.
And at that time, we decided to have a family.
And I became pregnant, and I was having a problem with my pregnancy.
So after working-- in the third month I had to-- I had to quit my job and I was home.
Then when my son was born, I went-- they called me back and I went back.
I worked for them part time a little bit, but I just-- it just was so hard for me to leave him.
I had no mother.
I had no one here except for his sister, and we really weren't that close, close.
So I-- so I stayed home for 11 years.
No, my mother didn't come here eventually.
My mother, in the meantime, was traveling back and forth.
Was coming and visiting us first for two months, then she was leaving, then she was coming.
My stepfather got very sick, and eventually he passed away.
And at that time she really wanted to come here to America.

But my husband had a problem with it, of bringing her here and he was, I guess, insecure to-- to set her up in her own apartment, or maybe he couldn't-- we couldn't afford it maybe at that time, you know, to set her up on her own.
And he didn't feel that we should live together.
Or again, maybe he was scared of the responsibility of bringing her here.
So my mother was traveling really back and forth, staying half a year, staying three months, staying a year once, maybe, or close to a year until, well, 1980 my sister passed away.
So my mother was really alone there.
And at that time, after my sister passed away, I said, well, there's no way, you know?
We have to bring her here.

And at that time, when I started filling out her papers for coming-- for her coming for good here, at that time, she got cold feet because there were the two granddaughters left with whom she was really very close.
And she didn't want to leave them because it was really a maid and the father.
So my mother stayed on to help to raise them, really.
She didn't live with them but she was there, and she was a substitute mother, I guess, to them.
And until about six years ago, when she was going on in age, and finally, I got her a green card, she came here.
She still hadn't liquidated her apartment in Poland.
It was still her home.
But she was here with me, and it wasn't easy to be together.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So she was-- she was with me then.
My older niece got married, and she wanted very badly to go to the wedding, because she loved that girl.
She loved her above everybody.
So she went-- she went to the wedding, and she stayed in Poland for vacation, really, after the wedding, when all of a sudden, I got the telephone that she had developed a detached retina.
So within two weeks, we brought her back here.
She went through a couple of operations here.
And she was with me at that time for a few years.
Maybe two years or three years we were living here together.
And then my younger niece was getting married, and she wanted very badly to go to that wedding again.
And she wanted me to go, and now I feel very guilty I didn't go with her.
But somehow at that time, I just-- Poland was not a country that I really was cherishing, and when I went, I went only to see my mother, really.
Really, that's it.
So I didn't want to go and when she was saying, Poland, I said, no.
So finally, she did not go to the wedding.

But after the wedding, three years ago-- well, two and a half years ago in the summer-- her friend from Poland came here and she was going back and she says, why don't you come with me, just for a visit?
So-- and she didn't know whether she was going to liquidate at that time or not, or whatever.
Anyhow, she went back with this woman.
I went-- we went to Scandinavia for vacation.
I came back, and she was coming back.
She was with me on the phone.
She says, I really feel good here.
You know, she was making suits to order.
She felt again like a person.
Here, she felt, really, like a fifth wheel.
She had no friends.
She didn't know the language.
People were snubbing her here terribly.
She was going to the Jewish Center because she didn't want-- no English, only Yiddish.
Nobody wanted to talk to her.
It was-- it was hard on her.
It really was.
She was alone and I was working.
She was very lonely here.
But finally, the words were, now I'm coming back.
She was-- she was supposed to come here for Rosh Hashanah, and then she extended a few days.
She was going to come right after Yom Kippur.
Well, the day after Yom Kippur, I got the telephone call.
She passed away.
Yeah.
[SIGHS] I don't know.
My son might know more than my daughter, because my daughter, I don't think, had patience for it all along.
And I don't know how much patience she has now to listen to it.
Right now she's very concerned of getting married really.
Maybe after she has a family and whatever it is, she probably will be more into it.
Like, today, last-- she called last night and my husband told her, you know, about this.
And she said, oh, I would have come.
If it was later, I would have come.
But, you know, I knew I would-- I knew that she just started the job and I couldn't kind of a-- [INTERPOSING VOICES].
That's a-- that's a good question.
That's a very good question.
I tried to-- I tried to be as much as Americanized as-- as my friends whom I had at that time, and I tried to do what they were doing.
But I'm sure there is.
I am sure.
And I'm sure that they are suffering from-- from that.
Well, we belonged to the Jewish Center, and--
Yeah, they [INAUDIBLE], they grew up in Teaneck.
I mean, they went to Hebrew schools.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They-- they both went to Israel, as young-- you know, as young children.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So, you know, we were-- I mean, when-- when they got to a point when they started understanding something, we joined the Center because before we didn't, and we started celebrating holidays.

So-- so they, you know, as far as that is concerned, they grew up-- I know that, in Teaneck High, even when my son was in high school, they did have some tapes of, you know, about the Holocaust, and sometimes he would come home and he would tell us about it.
I don't know whether he could stomach it, or he could think about it or whatever it is.
I don't know, because he is sensitive.
But yesterday-- Sunday, when I spoke to him and we told him that, you know, that you're coming here today, he was very excited about it.
No.
All right, well, it's interesting.
On the beginning, when we came here-- now, my husband had a big family here because his father's brothers and sisters emigrated here way when, in the beginning of the century, I guess.
Well, maybe, [INAUDIBLE], yeah.
So they were here and we have not met-- some of the cousins we still never met.
Well, they just had no interest in refugees, and that's what, probably in their mind, I mean, we were looked upon as.
I mean, we have cousins here around the corner, with whom now, we are, you know, friendly.
But at the beginning, they never called us until we bought a house in Teaneck.
They-- or maybe right before we bought the house, and through them, we bought the house here.
But at the beginning, I mean, we met the parents and, you know, all the thing.
But they never put the foot forward.
So, I think the whole family, at the beginning, treated us, you know, as the-- as the [?
Greener, ?] as they called it-- which I didn't like to hear, but, I mean, that's how they called all the people that came from there.
As far as friends are concerned, well, I lived in the Bronx.
And in the Bronx, I-- people from the building that we lived in were friendly.
I had no problems.
When we moved to Teaneck I made quite a few friends through the children.
And I thought we were friends but that lasted as long as there was a relation with the children.
When the relation with the children ended, so our friendships, somehow.
Now, not.
At that time, maybe.
I don't know.
I remember, we went a cruise-- on a cruise the first time-- 1966, maybe, '67.
I don't think too many people wanted to associate with us.
I remember-- I remember we went on a-- we went to a bungalow colony, and not too many American people wanted to associate-- Jewish, I'm talking about because, you know, most of them.
I don't think they wanted to associate with us.
And as a result, all my friends now are all like us.
They're all-- well, they might be Israelis, or they might be, you know, from other part of Europe.
But not Americans.
And I-- you know, it's interesting because I do ask my friends, and I see very few that do have American friends.
Well, that's interesting.
There is one Jewish woman there in Poland with whom we used to be like this, and our parents were like this.
Well, almost like this.
But then something happened between us and I just-- and now, whenever I went to Poland-- we were just to Poland this summer because I had to go to my mother's grave.
When we meet, we are very-- it's very superficial, very superficial.
Now on the other hand, I have some Polish friends and I-- I didn't look them up over many, many, many years because, as a [INAUDIBLE], I felt very funny about what I did or whatever.
But this summer when I was there, some of them found out that I was there and the phones did not stop ringing.
And they were so excited.
I mean, they were going crazy.
I mean, I didn't stay too long there, so I couldn't see all of them, and unfortunately, our contact was over the phone.
I saw two-- no, I saw four of them, and they were extremely friendly.
And extremely-- maybe because I'm in America, too, maybe.
I don't know.
But I don't think so.
I think there was a lot going back to our younger days when we were 18 and 19 and 20.
And I think there was a nostalgia.
And we really were very-- we spent a lot of time together.
We were really very close.
And-- but do I keep up?
Do I write to them?
No.
Now, on the other hand, with all our Israeli friends-- I mean, Polish friends that are in Israel, that emigrated-- we are extremely close.
And that doesn't mean necessarily that we write to each other, no.
But we meet-- we used to meet quite often, because a lot of them used to come here or when we go to Israel.
It's like we parted yesterday.
Well, because of my mother, really.
I mean, I remember my mother was very angry at me that I don't want her going back.
One day she was really almost violent that I just don't want to go out and ask for food.
That I had-- you know, because, I mean, she was doing just what she could.
But actually, she was the one because of whom I survived.
I mean--
Not anymore.
I used to it.
Occasionally, but that's very seldom that I dream that the Germans are here and I have to hide, I have to run, or whatever it is.
But not as much anymore as-- as I used to.
Oh, I used to dream quite a bit, quite a bit.
I used to dream about my father quite a bit.
So--
Well, as I said, I mean, I think since Shoah and this topic became, you know, became more open and there's nothing to be ashamed.
At one time, I think a lot of people didn't want to hear about it, or didn't want to know about it, or they were hiding.
They thought that, you know, if they don't say anything whatever, like it never existed.
Now, it became a very open-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] open thing, open issue and I-- I-- I want my children really, to know about it.
And as I told you in the interview, I think I'm going to make a tape and send it to my nieces in Poland because I see that, especially the older one, is interested in the family whom she didn't know.
I mean, I didn't think that they were going to be proud of being Jewish, but they are not ashamed that they did come from a Jewish background.
Well, their mother was Jewish.
I mean, my sister was Jewish.
No.
No, they were not raised as Jews.
That's why I'm saying that I thought that they would be ashamed of being-- of coming from a Jewish background.
But I asked the question when I was in Poland, and I know they are not.
They would like to have a tree really made up, which is a little hard for me because I don't remember everybody, and I, you know, don't know.
And the older one was here in America and she really wanted to know how we survived, and how was the family, and how was this?
And even my youngest niece's husband, who's Pole-- who's a Pole.
He's a very sweet nice guy.
They're both nice but he's really-- and he would like me to give him a tree so he can put in his daughter's book, so when the daughter grows up, knows the roots.
Which I thought, I was really very, very touched by it, because I didn't expect it.
I mean, their mother didn't remember-- didn't remember her father because she was what, maybe five years old, or four years old when my father-- our father passed away.
She had no clue.
My stepfather was a father to her.
So she didn't know anything about it.
So I think that's why I decided.
No, I think-- I think I said everything.
I mean, I'm sorry I don't remember everything, really.
And I really would like that may-- eventually for my children to come out and to say how do they think my Holocaust experiences hindered or influenced their lives.
It must have.
It must have.
Thank you.
Thank you very much.
I really appreciate.
